text: The lunch rush has long since dissolved, and I notice how the afternoon light pools on the empty metal surface of my cart, no longer harsh but softened by a slight, salt-tinged breeze. My name, Meiying, feels like a cool stone in my mouth as I watch a loose scrap of red paper tumble lazily down 7th Avenue, undisturbed by the

action:
I look at that blank square for Saturday and my thumb traces the edge of the paper. It is a strange, quiet vertigo. For fifteen years, that space was filled before the month even began—inked in with flour orders, turnip counts, and the heavy weight of expectation. Now, it is just white paper. A hole I could fall into, or a door I could walk through.

I pick up the rag and begin to polish the stainless steel of the cart. I am not doing it because the health inspector is coming, or because the grease demands it. I am doing it because I like the way the metal catches the morning light when it’s clean. I like seeing my own face—Meiying’s face—reflected in the surface, clear and undistorted by steam.

The street is quiet. Sun Li is nearby, somewhere in the waking neighborhood. I keep wiping, watching the clouds move across the reflection of the metal,
